source: Chhichhore (2019) — full screenplay; weighted to the 'loser' tag callback, the 'koshish decides, not result' hospital thesis, and the dual-timeline reunion
love_type: both — broad mainstream hit, AND a cult anti-suicide / 'failure is okay' text shared earnestly among students and parents (amplified by Sushant Singh Rajput's legacy)
core_emotion: the comforting permission to fail — that your worth isn't your result, and the warmth of friendships that outlast youth

dialogue_style.register: warm, jokey hostel-buddy banter that flips into earnest life-counsel; nostalgia-soaked, conversational
dialogue_style.rhythm: comic ensemble ribbing in the past timeline; gentle, direct sincerity in the present (hospital) timeline
dialogue_style.stylization: grounded crowd-pleaser realism; one clean thesis line engineered to be carried out as the takeaway
dialogue_style.why_it's_theirs: a story about 'losers' told by lovable losers — the self-deprecation earns the right to deliver its uplift without preaching

signature_dialogues.line: Tumhara result decide nahi karta ki tum loser ho ya nahi. Tumhari koshish decide karti hai. ...loser ki tarah nahi, fighter ki tarah jaao.
signature_dialogues.construction: reframe + imperative — separates worth from outcome, then converts it into a command for the listener's own life
signature_dialogues.the_setup: a father tells this to his son who attempted suicide after an exam failure; the whole nostalgic story was told to build to this
signature_dialogues.presentation: delivered bedside in the ICU as the emotional culmination of a two-hour story — the entire flashback structure exists to EARN this line, so it lands as proven wisdom rather than advice; staged at literal life-or-death stakes
signature_dialogues.payoff: the film's whole 'underdog gang' history retroactively becomes evidence for this one consolation
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: directly usable real-world reassurance for any student/parent facing failure; widely shared as anti-suicide comfort

signature_dialogues.line: Saale losers. / Loser hoga tera baap.
signature_dialogues.construction: an insult turned into an in-group badge of honour through repetition and reclamation
signature_dialogues.the_setup: the gang is mocked as losers; they adopt the slur as their team identity (shirts spelling LOSERS)
signature_dialogues.presentation: planted as a sting early, then repeated and reclaimed as a rallying chant — the callback structure converts humiliation into pride across the runtime
signature_dialogues.payoff: the word that wounded becomes the word that bonds — reclamation as catharsis
signature_dialogues.why_quotable: the reclaim-the-insult move is endlessly relatable; the line travels as friend-group shorthand

the_peaks.moment: the ICU thesis — 'result doesn't decide if you're a loser, your effort does'
the_peaks.iconic_line: loser ki tarah nahi, fighter ki tarah jaao.
the_peaks.why_loved: lands a real-world consolation millions needed to hear at literal life-and-death stakes

the_peaks.moment: the 'losers' gang winning (in spirit) the General Championship
the_peaks.why_loved: the mocked underdogs reclaiming their slur as glory — pure feel-good wish-fulfilment

wish_fulfillment: permission to have failed and still be okay; the dream of friendships that pick you up decades later exactly as they did at 19
character_magnetism: the lovable-loser gang (Acid, Sexa, Mummy, etc.) whose distinct quirks make the friend-group feel like one you belong to
audacity: framing its entire feel-good college comedy around a teenager's suicide attempt — staking laughs on a life-or-death frame and earning the tonal risk
portability: the 'koshish, not result' line as exam-season reassurance; 'losers' as reclaimed friend-group identity
why_cult_not_just_good: people send it to struggling students as actual emotional first-aid — it functions as advice, not just entertainment
transferable_love_triggers: Engineer the whole structure to EARN one consolation line, so the payoff feels like proven wisdom, not advice dropped in.
Reclaim an insult as an in-group badge through repetition — humiliation converted to pride is a deeply satisfying arc.
Deliver the thesis at literal life-or-death stakes so the comfort feels urgent and necessary, not preachy.
Build a friend-group of sharply distinct quirky 'losers' the audience wants to belong to — belonging is the engine of rewatch.
Frame a feel-good story around a genuinely heavy real-world problem (failure/suicide) so the warmth has weight and utility.
confidence: high — thesis and 'losers' callback confirmed verbatim in script
